response:
No, it is not possible to rotate these light sources to illuminate the entire plane.

To understand why, consider the total angular coverage provided by the six light sources. Each source illuminates a sector of $60^{\circ}$. With 6 sources, the maximum total angle covered is $6 \times 60^{\circ} = 360^{\circ}$. While this sum equals the full circle, this calculation assumes that the sectors can be arranged perfectly edge-to-edge without any gaps or overlaps around a single central point.

However, the problem specifies that the light sources are located at 6 *different* points on the plane. Because the sources are not all at the same location, their beams cannot form a continuous, gap-free ring that covers every direction from a single origin. Any attempt to align the beams to cover the plane will inevitably leave regions unlit. Specifically, for any point $P$ on the plane to be illuminated, it must lie within the $60^{\circ}$ sector of at least one source. If we consider the "shadow" or unlit region created by the geometry of the sources, the fact that they are distinct points means there will always be directions from certain points on the plane that are not covered by any of the beams, or conversely, there will be areas between the beams that remain dark.

More formally, if we consider the union of the illuminated sectors, the area covered is limited. Even if we could rotate them to cover a full $360^{\circ}$ around a specific central point, the light beams are rays originating from different locations. A ray from source $A$ and a ray from source $B$ cannot perfectly abut to cover a line segment unless they are collinear and overlapping, which wastes coverage. To cover the entire infinite plane, every point in the plane must be hit. It is a known result in geometry that $n$ sectors of angle $\alpha$ originating from distinct points cannot cover the entire plane if $n\alpha \le 360^{\circ}$, unless the points are coincident (which is forbidden here) or the sectors are larger. Since $6 \times 60^{\circ} = 360^{\circ}$, the "slack" required to handle the separation of the points is zero, making full coverage impossible. There will always be at least one point in the plane that lies in the gap between the beams.

P.S.
